content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CoriiIIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
March 12, 1999
Jeffrey G. Scott
Law Offices
16935 West Bemardo Drive, Suite 170
San Diego, California 92127
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-056
Dear Mr. Scott:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Director Ric Supple
regarding the provisions ofthe Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Please note that this letter
should not be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Section
1 8329(b)(8)(A).)
QUESTION
As a board member of the Desert Health Care District, will Director Supple have a
conflict of interest in a decision to provide funding to the Desert Hospital Foundation?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Director Supple will have a conflict of interest because, as a source of income of
$250 or more, the foundation is an economic interest ofthe director, and it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision to provide financial support to the foundation will have a material
financial effect on the foundation.
FACTS
You are the general counsel for the Desert Healthcare District (“district”). Ric Supple is
a director on the district’s board. The district is organized under the provisions of California
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-056
Page 2
Health and Safety Code section 32000 et seq., to provide health care for the area served by the
district in Riverside County. Effective May 30, 1997, the district leased Desert Hospital and
related assets to Tenet HealthSystems. As part ofthe transaction, the district became the sole
statutory member, as defined in California Corporations Code section 5056, ofthe Desert
Hospital Foundation (“foundation”), a nonprofit 501(c)(3) public benefit corporation.
Director Supple serves as a volunteer director on the foundation’s board. The
foundation’s articles of incorporation provide that the specific and primary purpose ofthe
foundation is to solicit, receive and maintain gifts ofmoney and property and to distribute money
and property to, and for the benefit of, the district. The district provides financial support to the
foundation. Director Supple has more than a 10 percent ownership interest in four radio stations
that serve the Palm Springs, California, area. In the last year, two ofthese radio stations have
received a total of approximately $17,000 from the foundation in advertising fees.
2
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making or in any way
attempting to use their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have
a financial interest. (Section 87100.) As a director on the district’s board, Mr. Supple is a public
official for purposes ofthe Act. (Section 82048.)
‘What are the official’s economic interests?
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on, among other enumerated “economic interests”: 1) any business
entity in which the official has an investment worth $1,000 or more; 2) any source of income of
$250 or more provided to, received by or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the
time the decision is made; and 3) any business entity in which the official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee or holds any position ofmanagement. (Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).)
Director Supple has more than a 10 percent ownership interest in four radio stations. As
the owner, Director Supple has an “economic interest” in the radio stations for purposes ofthe
Act. (Sections 87103(a), (c) and (d).)
As a member of the foundation’s board, Director Supple does not have an economic
interest in the board by virtue ofhis position because the foundation is not a “business entity” as
defined by the Act. (Sections 82005, 87103(d).) However, in the last year, two of the director’s
2 Other facts included in your request for advice related to past conduct. We do not provide advice
regarding past conduct. (Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(A).)


File No. A-99-056
Page 3
radio stations have received a total of approximately $17,000 from the foundation in advertising
fees. The term “income” includes a pro rata share of any income of a business entity in which
the official owns a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).) Since the foundation is a
source of income to him of $250 or more, the director has an also “economic interest” in the
foundation. (Section 87103(c).)
You have not indicated whether the foundation is a governmental agency. (See In re
Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.) The definition of “income” excludes salary and reimbursements
for expenses and per diem from governmental agencies. (Section 82030(b).) However, even if it
were determined that the foundation is a governmental agency, this income exception would not
apply. The governmental salary exception generally applies to income received as an employee
or consultant of a governmental agency, and thus would not apply to payments received in
exchange for advertising services provided by the radio stations. (Rainey Advice Letter, No. A-
98-101.)
Accordingly, Director Supple may not make, participate in making, or use his official
position to influence a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on his business or on the foundation. Your question specifically
involves the foundation. Therefore, the remainder of this letter will focus only on the director’s
economic interest in the foundation as a source of income.
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision wifi have a material fmancial effect on
the official’s economic interest?
Once the official identifies his or her economic interest, the official must evaluate
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material financial
effect on the economic interest. First, the official must determine whether the economic interest
will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. Based upon the type of involvement, the
official must then apply the appropriate regulatory standard to ascertain whether the financial
impact of the decision will be material. Afler the official finds the pertinent materiality standard,
he or she must decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be
met.
1. Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A person who is a source of income is directly involved in a decision if the person is the
named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) A person is the
subject of a decision if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit or contract with the person. (Id.) If a person is not directly involved in the
decision, the person is indirectly involved for purposes of applying the materiality standards.
Since the foundation would be the named party in a decision to allocate funds to the foundation,
the foundation is considered to be directly involved in the decision.


File No. A-99-056
Page 4
2. What is the appropriate materiality standard?
When a person who is a source of income is directly involved in a decision, any
reasonably foreseeable effect of the decision is deemed to be material.
3 (Regulation 18705.3(a).)
3. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. (Regulation 18706.) When the effect of a decision is deemed to be material under
the pertinent materiality regulation, as in this case, the official must determine whether it is
substantially likely that any financial effect will occur as a result of the decision. (Regulation
1 8700(b)(6).) This is a “one-penny rule”—if any financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, the
official will have a disqualifying financial interest. Since a decision to provide funding to the
foundation will have some financial effect on the foundation, it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the foundation. Accordingly, Director Supple
may not make, participate in making or use his official position to influence a decision to
distribute funds to the foundation.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JB :tls
Enclosure
On the other hand, when a person who is a source of income is indirectly involved in a decision and that
person is a nonprofit entity, the appropriate standard to determine materiality is contained in Regulation
18705.3(b)(2) (copy enclosed).

As a board member of the Desert Health Care District, will Director Supple have a
conflict of interest in a decision to provide funding to the Desert Hospital Foundation?

$250 or more, the foundation is an economic interest ofthe director, and it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision to provide financial support to the foundation will have a material
financial effect on the foundation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As a board member of the Desert Health Care District, will Director Supple have a
conflict of interest in a decision to provide funding to the Desert Hospital Foundation?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Director Supple will have a conflict of interest because, as a source of income of
$250 or more, the foundation is an economic interest ofthe director, and it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision to provide financial support to the foundation will have a material
financial effect on the foundation.
